results.title: [Literature Review] FORT-Searcher: Synthesizing Shortcut-Resistant Search Tasks for Training Deep Search Agents
results.content:
Experiments demonstrate FORT-Searcher's effectiveness. Using Qwen3-30B-A3B as a base model and SFT, FORT-Searcher achieves the best overall performance among comparable-size open-source agents on challenging deep search benchmarks like BrowseComp, BrowseComp-ZH, xbench-DeepSearch, and Seal-0. For instance, on BrowseComp, it scores 72.2, outperforming MiroThinker-1.7-mini (67.9) and Qwen3.5-35B-A3B (61.0). It also shows strong competitiveness against much larger open-source models and even some proprietary agents, particularly on BrowseComp-ZH where it achieves the best open-source result (75.0).

Further analyses support FORT's design principles: [...] The synthesized trajectories are used to train FORT-Searcher, an SFT-only deep search agent. At inference, FORT-Searcher uses a context-managed search protocol that retains tool-call results and restarts trajectories (clears history) if a predefined turn limit is reached. [...] The paper introduces a shortcut-aware difficulty framework for multi-constraint agentic retrieval, which models deep search difficulty as depending on both the intended task structure and how the question is realized in the retrieval environment and by the solver. A task instance is defined as $\mathbf{q} = (\mathcal{X}, \mathcal{C}{\mathbf{q}}, \Sigma)$, where $\mathcal{X}$ is the answer space, $\mathcal{C}{\mathbf{q}}$ is the set of constraints, and $\Sigma$ is the retrieval interface. $\text{Ans}(P)$ denotes the candidate pool after applying constraints $P \subseteq \mathcal{C}{\mathbf{q}}$, with the gold answer being $\mathbf{y}^{\star}$ such that $\text{Ans}(\mathcal{C}{\mathbf{q}}) = {\mathbf{y}^{\star}}$.

results.title: [PDF] FORT-Searcher: Synthesizing Shortcut-Resistant Search Tasks for ...
results.content: Our methodology has two components. First, FORT synthesizes shortcut-resistant questions and search trajectories by controlling how constraints, evidence sources, and intermediate dependencies are con-structed before the final question is exposed to a solver. Second, the resulting trajectories are used to train FORT-Searcher, an SFT-only search agent with a context-managed inference protocol. This separation links the data-side objective of reducing route-level and solver-level shortcuts to the model-side objective of learning robust multi-turn evidence acquisition. [...] Experiments show that FORT produces useful supervision for deep search agents. With supervised fine-tuning alone, we introduce FORT-Searcher, which achieves the best overall performance among comparable-size open-source agents on challenging benchmarks, including BrowseComp, BrowseComp-ZH, xbench-DeepSearch, and Seal-0. Further analyses show that FORT induces longer pre-answer search than existing open-source deep search datasets and reduces multiple shortcut patterns, supporting the importance of shortcut-resistant data synthesis. [...] 4.2. Main Results Table 6 compares FORT-Searcher with proprietary, large-scale open-source, and comparable-size open-source search agents. Among comparable-size open-source agents, FORT-Searcher achieves the highest overall score of 66.2, outperforming MiroThinker-1.7-mini  by 1.6 points and Qwen3.5-35B-A3B  by 6.3 points. It ranks first on BrowseComp, BrowseComp-ZH, and xbench-DeepSearch-2505, and ties the best result on xbench-DeepSearch-2510. On BrowseComp, it improves over MiroThinker-1.7-mini from 67.9 to 72.2. On BrowseComp-ZH, it improves from 72.3 to 75.0. On xbench-DeepSearch-2505, it reaches 80.8, outperforming the strongest comparable-size baseline, OpenSeekerV2, by 2.8 points.

results.title: FORT-Searcher: Synthesizing Shortcut-Resistant Search Tasks for Training Deep Search Agents
results.content:
Experiments show that FORT produces useful supervision for
deep search agents. With supervised fine-tuning alone, we introduce
FORT-Searcher, which achieves the best overall performance among
comparable-size open-source agents on challenging benchmarks, including
BrowseComp, BrowseComp-ZH, xbench-DeepSearch, and Seal-0. Further analyses show
that FORT induces longer pre-answer search than existing open-source
deep search datasets and reduces multiple shortcut patterns, supporting the
importance of shortcut-resistant data synthesis.

Our contributions are as follows: [...] Our methodology has two components. First, FORT synthesizes
shortcut-resistant questions and search trajectories by controlling how
constraints, evidence sources, and intermediate dependencies are constructed
before the final question is exposed to a solver. Second, the resulting
trajectories are used to train FORT-Searcher, an SFT-only search agent
with a context-managed inference protocol. This separation links the data-side
objective of reducing route-level and solver-level shortcuts to the model-side
objective of learning robust multi-turn evidence acquisition.

### 3.1 FORT: Shortcut-Resistant Data Synthesis [...] At inference time, FORT-Searcher uses a context-managed search
protocol. Within each rollout, retrieved tool-call results are retained so that
the model can reuse accumulated evidence. If the model reaches the predefined
turn limit without producing a final answer, the current interaction history is
cleared and the agent restarts from the original question.

## 4 Experiment

results.title: Daily Paper Cast - Podcast - Apple Podcasts
results.content:
difficulty framework and identify four actionable shortcut risks: evidence co-coverage, single-clue selectivity, exposed constants, and prior-knowledge binding. To diagnose their realized effects, we use trajectory signatures including solving cost, answer hit time, and prior-shortcut rate. Guided by this framework, we introduce FORT, a Framework of Shortcut-Resistant Training-Data Synthesis. FORT constructs shortcut-resistant training data by controlling shortcut risks across entity selection, evidence graph construction, question formulation, and adversarial refinement. Experiments show that FORT induces longer pre-answer search and fewer shortcut patterns than existing open-source deep search datasets. Using the resulting trajectories, we train FORT-Searcher with supervised fine-tuning [...] 🤗 Upvotes: 71 | cs.CL Authors: Jia Deng, Yimeng Chen, Xiaoqing Xiang, Ziyang Zeng, Shuo Tang, Wayne Xin Zhao, Feng Chang, Chuan Hao, Yuan Wei, Ran Tao, Bryan Dai, Ji-Rong Wen Title: FORT-Searcher: Synthesizing Shortcut-Resistant Search Tasks for Training Deep Search Agents Arxiv:  Abstract: Training deep search agents requires verifiable questions whose answers remain unavailable until sufficient evidence has been acquired through search. Existing synthesis methods often increase apparent difficulty by enriching graph structures, but structural complexity alone does not guarantee realized search difficulty: the intended search process can collapse through a cheaper identifying route. We formalize this gap with a shortcut-aware difficulty framework and identify four actionable shortcut [...] 1d ago

### FORT-Searcher: Synthesizing Shortcut-Resistant Search Tasks for Training Deep Search Agents

results.title: The Dunster House BalconyFort Searcher Climbing Frame - Climbing Frame Installer
results.content:
The Dunster House BalconyFort Searcher features a spacious wooden play fort with a play deck and a railed balcony area for kids to climb to plus an enclosed lower play cabin beneath the play deck, a rockwall ladder, a slide and even a combined monkey bar swing beam. Dunster House climbing frames are manufactures from a natural looking timber, which is darker than play frames from brands such as Play Crazy, Action and Jungle Gym for example, however does not have the same look as Selwood Products climbing frames which have a distinctive copper colour. [...] Courtesy of Dunster House 

Courtesy of Dunster House

The BalconyFort Searcher climbing frame from Dunster House features a solid wooden play fort with a play deck to climb to using the rockwall ladder at the back of the play frame. The play deck is enclosed around the outside and sheltered by a roof which provides protection from harsh sunlight too. Kids can stay cool in the play fort even on hot sunny days as the roof will shade the play deck. Joined to the play deck is the railed balcony box which gives this playset its name. The Balcony is a fun accessory which expands the play deck and means more children can play in the play fort at once. [...] Last but not least is the combined monkey bar swing beam to the side of the play fort. The monkey bar swing set is a great way of saving space in your garden but also gives children more to keep them amused. The monkey bars are a fantastic activity for improving strength and coordination and are a challenging accessory for older kids especially to enjoy testing themselves. The BalconyFort Searcher includes two plastic moulded swings for kids to play on which are a brilliant addition to any playset.

For more information visit www.dunsterhouse.co.uk.

results.title: AI Agents of the Week: Papers You Should Know About - LLM Watch
results.content: The “Shortcut” Illusion - Why Your Benchmarks Are Lying to You: The most consequential finding this week is that outcome-only evaluation substantially overestimates agent performance. WeaveBench introduces a trajectory-aware judge that catches agents fabricating visual evidence and using hard-coded metrics - behaviors invisible to traditional pass/fail grading. The best frontier model-runtime pairing achieves only 41.2% PassRate on its 114 real-world tasks. Meanwhile, FORT-Searcher demonstrates that structurally complex search tasks often collapse through “cheaper identifying routes,” and proposes a shortcut-aware difficulty framework that forces agents to actually reason rather than exploit dataset artifacts. Together, these papers suggest that a significant portion of reported agent

results.title: Dunster House Balcony Fort Searcher – The Best Outdoor Playsets
results.content:
Skip to content 

The Best Outdoor Playsets

If you were a child, you would want one of these

# Dunster House Balcony Fort Searcher

### Wooden Climbing Frame BalconyFort Searcher Wooden Children’s Outdoor Play Tower with Monkey Bars, Swings & Slide – Dunster House

Available on Amazon.co.uk here.

This is a piece of quality engineering for your garden.  It is solidly built, reliable and independently safety tested.

It’s suitable for 3 years to 14 years (up to 50 Kilos).  The easy climb ladder are good for three years up with the monkey bars being more challenging for older children, but you know the next time you have a party outside the adults will be on it after a few BBQ chicken legs / Beers / Prosecco (delete as appropriate)

Features at a glance: – [...] Features at a glance: –

 Monkey Bars
 Slide
 Two swings
 Climbing Wall / Ladder
 Covered Den Area
 BalconyBalcony Fort Searcher

Image courtesy  of Amazon.co.uk

Look at the reviews on Amazon.co.uk

It’s a reasonable size so check it fits your space before ordering:-

Width 4.6m (15’3″) x Depth 3.8m (12’5″) x Height 2.8mm (9’2″)

All sizes are approximate. [...] All sizes are approximate.

It neutral colours complement your average garden setting.  I think you will agree that this looks good and won’t be an eyesore in your garden, but I believe it’s the details that set this apart.  The main structure is made of pressure treated timber and is guaranteed for 10 years.  It will last the test of time.   As a minimum you will have it for 10 years; dividing the price over its minimum lifetime is roughly £68 a year, that’s a bargain for something like this.
